quizQuestion: Which urban geography term refers to the physical characteristics of the land on which a city is built (terrain, water access)?
explanation: Site refers to the absolute physical characteristics of a city's location — its terrain, soil, water supply, and topography. Situation refers to the city's location relative to other places. Both influence a city's origin and growth.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.A.1: Site and situation influence the origin, function, and growth of cities.

quizQuestion: A city's location relative to surrounding regions, transportation routes, and other cities is called its {___}.
explanation: Situation describes a city's relative location — its connectivity and relationship to other places. New York's situation is excellent: at the mouth of the Hudson River, connected to interior markets. Situation is often more important than site for long-term urban growth.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.A.1: Site and situation influence the origin, function, and growth of cities.

quizQuestion: A city with a population of at least 10 million people is classified as a {___}.
explanation: Megacities are urban agglomerations with populations exceeding 10 million. Examples include Tokyo, Mumbai, and Lagos. The CED notes that megacities and metacities are increasingly located in the periphery and semiperiphery, not just in wealthy core nations.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.A.3: Megacities and metacities are distinct spatial outcomes of urbanization increasingly located in countries of the periphery and semiperiphery.

quizQuestion: Which term describes an urban agglomeration with a population exceeding 20 million people?
explanation: Metacities have populations above 20 million. Tokyo is currently the world's largest metacity. The CED distinguishes metacities from megacities (10M+) as especially large spatial outcomes of urbanization. Both are increasingly found in the Global South.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.A.3: Megacities and metacities are distinct spatial outcomes of urbanization increasingly located in countries of the periphery and semiperiphery.

quizQuestion: A large node of office and retail activity on the urban fringe with more jobs than bedrooms is called an {___}.
explanation: Edge cities emerge at the periphery of older metropolitan areas, often near highway interchanges. They have suburban locations but urban-scale employment. The term was popularized by journalist Joel Garreau. Examples include Tysons Corner, Virginia.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.A.4: Processes of suburbanization, sprawl, and decentralization have created new land-use forms — including edge cities, exurbs, and boomburbs.

quizQuestion: Residential communities beyond the suburbs that are largely bedroom communities for distant city workers are called {___}.
explanation: Exurbs lie beyond the suburbs, typically with lower density and longer commutes to urban employment centers. Residents are often higher-income households seeking larger lots. Unlike edge cities, exurbs lack significant employment nodes of their own.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.A.4: Processes of suburbanization, sprawl, and decentralization have created new land-use forms — including edge cities, exurbs, and boomburbs.

quizQuestion: Cities that function at the top of the world's urban hierarchy and drive globalization are called {___}.
explanation: World cities (also called global cities) serve as command-and-control centers of the global economy. London, New York, and Tokyo are tier-one world cities. They house headquarters of multinational corporations, major financial exchanges, and international organizations.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.B.1: World cities function at the top of the world's urban hierarchy and drive globalization.

quizQuestion: Which theory, developed by Walter Christaller, explains the size and spacing of cities using hexagonal market areas?
explanation: Christaller's Central Place Theory (1933) argues that settlements exist to provide goods and services to surrounding populations. Larger settlements offer higher-order goods with greater range and threshold. The theory predicts a hexagonal lattice pattern of market areas across an isotropic plain.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.C.1: Principles useful for explaining the distribution and size of cities include rank-size rule, the primate city, gravity model, and Christaller's central place theory.

quizQuestion: Which geographer developed Central Place Theory, proposing that settlements are distributed in hexagonal market areas based on threshold and range?
explanation: Walter Christaller (1893-1969) was a German geographer who published Central Place Theory in 1933. His model explained urban hierarchy using two key concepts: threshold (minimum population needed to support a service) and range (maximum distance consumers travel for a service).
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.C.1: Principles useful for explaining the distribution and size of cities include Christaller's central place theory.

quizQuestion: In Central Place Theory, the minimum number of customers needed to make a business economically viable is called the {___}.
explanation: Threshold is the minimum market size (population) needed to support a central place function. Low-order goods (convenience stores) have low thresholds; high-order goods (specialty hospitals) require large thresholds. Threshold and range together determine where services locate.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.C.1: Christaller's central place theory explains city size and distribution using threshold and range.

quizQuestion: In Central Place Theory, the maximum distance a customer will travel to obtain a good or service is called the {___}.
explanation: Range is the outer limit of a central place's market area — beyond this distance, customers will seek a closer alternative. High-order goods (e.g., major surgery) have large ranges; low-order goods (e.g., a loaf of bread) have small ranges. Range and threshold together define a service area.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.C.1: Christaller's central place theory explains city size and distribution using threshold and range.

quizQuestion: The principle that a country's cities will be ranked by population so that the second city is 1/2 the size of the largest, the third is 1/3, etc. is the {___}.
explanation: The rank-size rule (Zipf's Law applied to cities) predicts a regular, hierarchical distribution of city sizes. In countries that follow the rule (e.g., United States), the relationship between rank and size is proportional. Countries dominated by one very large city deviate from this pattern.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.C.1: Principles useful for explaining the distribution and size of cities include rank-size rule.

quizQuestion: A country's largest city that is disproportionately larger than all other cities and dominates economic and cultural life is called a {___}.
explanation: A primate city is at least twice the population of the second largest city and dominates economic, political, and cultural functions. Examples include Paris (France), Bangkok (Thailand), and Mexico City (Mexico). Countries with primate cities often deviate from the rank-size rule.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.C.1: Principles useful for explaining the distribution and size of cities include the primate city.

quizQuestion: Which model predicts that the interaction between two cities is proportional to their populations and inversely proportional to distance?
explanation: The Gravity Model (based on Newton's law of gravitation) predicts the degree of spatial interaction between two places. Larger populations increase interaction; greater distance decreases it. It is used to predict migration, trade flows, commuting patterns, and city-to-city connections.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.C.1: Principles useful for explaining the distribution and size of cities include the gravity model.

quizQuestion: Which urban land-use model depicts cities as a series of concentric rings expanding outward from the Central Business District?
explanation: Ernest Burgess developed this model based on 1920s Chicago. Five concentric rings: Zone 1 (CBD), Zone 2 (Zone in Transition / light manufacturing), Zone 3 (working-class housing), Zone 4 (middle-class residential), Zone 5 (commuter zone / suburbs). Criticized for reflecting only early 20th-century American cities.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.D.1: Models and theories useful for explaining internal structures of cities include the Burgess concentric-zone model.

quizQuestion: Which geographer developed the Concentric-Zone Model using 1920s Chicago as a template for urban land-use patterns?
explanation: Ernest Burgess (1886–1966) was a University of Chicago sociologist-geographer who proposed the concentric zone model in 1925. Chicago's industrial growth and immigrant neighborhoods made it an ideal case study. His model described how cities expand outward from a central core in distinct rings.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.D.1: Models and theories useful for explaining internal structures of cities include the Burgess concentric-zone model.

quizQuestion: Which urban land-use model argues that cities grow in wedge-shaped sectors radiating outward from the CBD along transportation routes?
explanation: Homer Hoyt's 1939 Sector Model revised Burgess by showing that land uses extend outward in pie-shaped sectors, not concentric rings. High-income housing develops along scenic routes and away from industrial sectors. Transportation corridors (rail lines, highways) shape the direction of growth.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.D.1: Models and theories useful for explaining internal structures of cities include the Hoyt sector model.

quizQuestion: Which urban land-use model, developed by Harris and Ullman, argues that cities have multiple centers of activity rather than a single CBD?
explanation: Chauncy Harris and Edward Ullman proposed the Multiple-Nuclei Model in 1945. Cities develop around multiple distinct nodes — the CBD, industrial district, university campus, airport — rather than a single center. This better reflects post-WWII American cities shaped by the automobile.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.D.1: Models and theories useful for explaining internal structures of cities include the Harris and Ullman multiple-nuclei model.

quizQuestion: Which urban model describes a dispersed, automobile-dependent city with multiple peripheral employment and retail nodes linked by highways?
explanation: The Galactic City Model (also called the Peripheral Model) reflects late 20th-century American urban form. The CBD loses dominance as edge cities, malls, and office parks develop along beltways. The pattern resembles a galaxy with scattered nodes of activity around a weakened core.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.D.1: Models and theories useful for explaining internal structures of cities include the galactic city model.

quizQuestion: Which urban land-use model features a commercial spine extending from the CBD, elite housing along the spine, and a disamenity zone on the periphery?
explanation: The Griffin-Ford Latin American City Model depicts a spine of commercial activity and high-income housing radiating from the CBD, surrounded by a zone of maturity, an inner city zone of accretion, and a disamenity zone (squatter settlements) on the outer fringe. This contrasts with North American models where wealthy residents suburbanize outward.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.D.1: Models and theories useful for explaining internal structures of cities include urban models drawn from Latin America.

quizQuestion: Which urban land-use model, developed to describe Sub-Saharan African cities, features three CBDs (colonial, traditional, and market) reflecting a legacy of colonial urban planning?
explanation: The African City Model (associated with de Blij) depicts three distinct CBDs: the colonial CBD (European-built), the traditional/ethnic CBD, and the informal/market CBD. Mining, manufacturing, and squatter settlements surround these cores. The structure reflects colonial spatial policies that segregated populations by ethnicity.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.D.1: Models and theories useful for explaining internal structures of cities include urban models drawn from Africa.

quizQuestion: Which urban model describes a port zone as the dominant center, surrounded by a commercial zone and ethnic quarters radiating outward, reflecting colonial-era Southeast Asian cities?
explanation: T.G. McGee's Southeast Asian City Model places a colonial port zone at the center rather than a traditional CBD. An alien commercial zone (often Chinese merchants) and government quarters surround it. This model reflects the colonial history of cities like Singapore, Jakarta, and Hanoi, shaped by trade and European administration.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.D.1: Models and theories useful for explaining internal structures of cities include urban models drawn from Southeast Asia.

quizQuestion: The urban theory that explains how land values and land use change with distance from the city center — highest rents near CBD, declining outward — is called {___}.
explanation: Bid-rent theory (applied to urban areas) shows that different users — commercial, industrial, residential — compete for land based on their willingness to pay. Commercial uses can pay the most near the CBD; residential uses tolerate greater distance. The result is a predictable gradient of land use from CBD outward.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.D.1: Models and theories useful for explaining internal structures of cities include bid-rent theory.

quizQuestion: A zoning approach that combines residential, commercial, and office uses within the same district or building is called {___}.
explanation: Mixed land use integrates residential, retail, office, and civic functions within walkable proximity. It contrasts with single-use (Euclidean) zoning that separates these activities into distinct areas. The CED identifies mixed land use as a key component of smart growth and New Urbanism.
sourceDetails: CED EK IMP-6.C.1: Sustainable design initiatives and zoning practices include mixed land use.

quizQuestion: According to the CED, a city's {___} — including transportation, water, sewage, and power systems — directly affects its spatial patterns of economic and social development.
explanation: Infrastructure is the foundational network of physical systems (roads, transit, water, sewer, broadband, power) that enables urban economic and social activity. High-quality infrastructure attracts investment and supports dense, walkable development; poor infrastructure perpetuates poverty and inequality.
sourceDetails: CED EK IMP-6.B.1: The location and quality of a city's infrastructure directly affects its spatial patterns of economic and social development.

quizQuestion: Mixed-use development concentrated within a walkable area around a transit station is called {___}.
explanation: Transit-oriented development (TOD) clusters high-density housing, offices, and retail within a quarter-mile walking radius of rail or bus rapid transit stations. It reduces car dependence, supports ridership, and creates walkable urban environments. The CED lists TOD as a sustainable design initiative.
sourceDetails: CED EK IMP-6.C.1: Sustainable design initiatives and zoning practices include transportation-oriented development.

quizQuestion: Which urban planning movement advocates for walkable neighborhoods, mixed uses, transit access, and traditional town design to reduce sprawl?
explanation: New Urbanism is a planning movement that emerged in the 1980s-90s to counter suburban sprawl. It promotes compact, walkable neighborhoods with grid street patterns, mixed housing types, front porches, and nearby transit. Celebrated examples include Seaside, Florida. Critics note it can raise housing costs and create exclusionary enclaves.
sourceDetails: CED EK IMP-6.C.1: Sustainable design initiatives and zoning practices include New Urbanism.

quizQuestion: Which urban planning policy framework promotes compact development, infill, mixed uses, and transit to limit sprawl and protect open space?
explanation: Smart growth is a broad policy approach including urban growth boundaries, infill incentives, mixed-use zoning, and transit investment to channel urban growth inward rather than outward. It is supported by the EPA and many state governments as an alternative to unmanaged sprawl.
sourceDetails: CED EK IMP-6.C.1: Sustainable design initiatives and zoning practices include smart-growth policies, including New Urbanism, greenbelts, and slow-growth cities.

quizQuestion: A protected ring of open land (parks, farms, or forests) surrounding a city, designated to prevent urban sprawl, is called a {___}.
explanation: Greenbelts are legally protected open spaces around cities that prevent outward urban expansion. London's greenbelt, established in 1938, is the most famous. Critics argue greenbelts can inflate housing prices inside the city. The CED lists greenbelts as a smart-growth response to urban sprawl.
sourceDetails: CED EK IMP-6.C.1: Sustainable design initiatives and zoning practices include greenbelts.

quizQuestion: The discriminatory practice of denying mortgages, insurance, or other financial services to residents of minority neighborhoods — often literally outlined in red on maps — is called {___}.
explanation: Redlining was formally institutionalized in the 1930s by the Home Owners' Loan Corporation (HOLC), which graded neighborhoods and denied federally backed mortgages to Black and immigrant communities marked in red. The practice was outlawed by the Fair Housing Act of 1968 but created lasting patterns of urban disinvestment.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.A.1: Issues related to housing and housing discrimination such as redlining, blockbusting, and affordability affect urban populations.

quizQuestion: The practice of encouraging white homeowners to sell quickly by warning that minority groups are moving into the neighborhood — driving down prices — is called {___}.
explanation: Blockbusting was practiced by real estate agents who would profit by inducing white homeowners to sell cheaply (through fear of integration), then reselling or renting at inflated prices to Black families desperate for housing in any neighborhood. It accelerated racial residential segregation and is now illegal.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.A.1: Issues related to housing and housing discrimination such as redlining, blockbusting, and affordability affect urban populations.

quizQuestion: The process by which wealthier residents move into older urban neighborhoods, renovating properties and raising land values while displacing lower-income residents, is called {___}.
explanation: Gentrification transforms disinvested urban neighborhoods by attracting higher-income residents who renovate buildings and bring new businesses. It revitalizes neglected areas but often displaces long-term residents who can no longer afford rising rents. The CED notes both positive and negative consequences of gentrification.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.A.4: Urban renewal and gentrification have both positive and negative consequences.

quizQuestion: The large-scale migration of white residents from racially mixed urban areas to predominantly white suburbs, often in response to racial integration, is called {___}.
explanation: White flight describes the post-WWII phenomenon of white middle-class families leaving cities for suburbs, accelerated by blockbusting, integration of schools, and suburbanization policy. It reduced the tax base of inner cities, worsening public services, and reinforced racial residential segregation.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.A.1: As urban populations move within a city, economic and social challenges result, including issues related to housing discrimination.

quizQuestion: Government programs that demolish deteriorated urban areas and replace them with new housing, commercial facilities, or public spaces are called {___}.
explanation: Urban renewal in the 1950s-70s used federal funds to clear 'slums' (often minority neighborhoods) and replace them with highways, public housing, or commercial development. Critics called it 'Negro removal.' The CED notes both positive outcomes (modernized infrastructure) and negative consequences (community destruction, displacement).
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.A.4: Urban renewal and gentrification have both positive and negative consequences.

quizQuestion: Informal housing areas built on land without legal ownership, typically lacking water, sewage, and electricity, found on the urban periphery in developing countries, are called {___}.
explanation: Squatter settlements (favelas in Brazil, shantytowns, barrios marginales) form when rural migrants cannot afford formal housing. Residents build homes on unused land — often hazardous land unsuited for legal development. The CED notes that squatter settlements and conflicts over land tenure are growing challenges in large cities worldwide.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.A.2: Squatter settlements and conflicts over land tenure within large cities have increased.

quizQuestion: An area of the worst housing in a city — abandoned buildings, extreme poverty, and social disorder — that lacks access to basic services is called a {___}.
explanation: Disamenity zones (zones of abandonment) are found in both the developed and developing world — Detroit's abandoned inner neighborhoods or the outer periphery of Latin American cities. The CED cites their growth as a consequence of urban economic and social challenges including housing discrimination and disinvestment.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.A.1: Issues related to housing and housing discrimination result in the growth of disamenity zones or zones of abandonment.

quizQuestion: An area — often low-income and urban — where residents have limited or no access to affordable, nutritious food is called a {___}.
explanation: Food deserts result from the absence of grocery stores in low-income neighborhoods, leaving residents dependent on fast food and convenience stores. They are linked to health disparities. The USDA defines a food desert as a low-income area where more than one-third of residents live more than one mile from a supermarket.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.A.1: Issues related to housing and housing discrimination include access to services; challenges include environmental injustice.

quizQuestion: The disproportionate burden of environmental hazards (pollution, toxic waste sites, flooding) on low-income communities and communities of color is called {___}.
explanation: Environmental injustice occurs when marginalized communities bear greater exposure to pollution, industrial hazards, and climate risks than wealthier communities. Studies show that hazardous waste facilities are disproportionately sited near low-income and minority neighborhoods. The CED lists environmental injustice as a key urban challenge.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.A.1: Issues include rising crime, environmental injustice, and the growth of disamenity zones.

quizQuestion: The phenomenon where an urban area is significantly warmer than its surrounding rural areas due to human activities, dark surfaces, and reduced vegetation is called the {___}.
explanation: Urban heat islands form because asphalt, concrete, and buildings absorb and retain solar radiation, while less vegetation means less evaporative cooling. Cities can be 2-5°F warmer than surrounding rural areas. This worsens air conditioning demand, air quality, and heat-related mortality in low-income neighborhoods.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.B.1: Challenges to urban sustainability include air and water quality and climate change impacts.

quizQuestion: A legally defined line that restricts urban development beyond its border, used to prevent sprawl and protect agricultural land, is called an {___}.
explanation: Urban growth boundaries (UGBs) are adopted by municipalities to concentrate development inside the boundary and preserve farmland and open space outside. Portland, Oregon's UGB is the most studied example in the United States. The CED identifies UGBs as a response to urban sustainability challenges.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.B.2: Responses to urban sustainability challenges can include establishment of urban growth boundaries.

quizQuestion: A former industrial or commercial site contaminated by pollution that requires environmental cleanup before redevelopment is called a {___}.
explanation: Brownfields are abandoned or underused industrial or commercial properties where expansion or redevelopment is complicated by real or perceived environmental contamination. Brownfield remediation and redevelopment is a smart-growth strategy that recycles urban land rather than expanding into undeveloped areas.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.B.2: Responses to urban sustainability challenges can include remediation and redevelopment of brownfields.

quizQuestion: The uncontrolled outward expansion of urban areas into surrounding rural land, characterized by low-density housing and car dependence, is called {___}.
explanation: Urban sprawl converts farmland and natural habitat into low-density subdivisions, strip malls, and highways at the urban fringe. It increases vehicle miles traveled, carbon emissions, and infrastructure costs while reducing walkability. The CED identifies sprawl as both a product of suburbanization and a challenge to urban sustainability.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.B.1: Challenges to urban sustainability include suburban sprawl and the large ecological footprint of cities.

quizQuestion: The population movement from urban cores to surrounding residential areas, made possible by automobiles, highways, and affordable mortgages, is called {___}.
explanation: Suburbanization accelerated in the U.S. after WWII, driven by GI Bill mortgages, the Interstate Highway System, and zoning laws separating residential from commercial land. The CED links suburbanization to the creation of edge cities, exurbs, and boomburbs, and to challenges like urban sprawl and city disinvestment.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.A.4: Processes of suburbanization, sprawl, and decentralization have created new land-use forms.

quizQuestion: A rapidly growing suburban city with populations above 100,000 that maintains a suburban character despite its size is called a {___}.
explanation: Boomburbs are defined as places with over 100,000 residents that have maintained double-digit population growth in recent decades but are not central cities of metropolitan areas. Examples include Mesa, Arizona and Henderson, Nevada. They represent a distinct form of urban growth that blurs city-suburb distinctions.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.A.4: Processes of suburbanization, sprawl, and decentralization have created new land-use forms — including edge cities, exurbs, and boomburbs.

quizQuestion: A zoning policy that requires or incentivizes developers to include a percentage of affordable housing units in new market-rate residential projects is called {___}.
explanation: Inclusionary zoning mandates that a share (typically 10-20%) of new residential units be priced affordably for low- and moderate-income households. The CED lists it as one response to urban economic and social challenges. Critics argue it can reduce overall housing supply by discouraging development.
sourceDetails: CED EK SPS-6.A.3: Responses to economic and social challenges in urban areas can include inclusionary zoning and local food movements.

quizQuestion: A key spatial pattern in modern urbanization is that megacities are increasingly located in countries of the {___} rather than wealthy core nations.
explanation: The CED explicitly notes that megacities and metacities are increasingly located in peripheral and semiperipheral countries (India, China, Nigeria, Brazil, Bangladesh). This contrasts with earlier urbanization dominated by core (MDC) cities. Cities like Lagos, Dhaka, and Kinshasa are growing faster than any European or North American city.
sourceDetails: CED EK PSO-6.A.3: Megacities and metacities are distinct spatial outcomes of urbanization increasingly located in countries of the periphery and semiperiphery.
